name: Buckskin
short_description: BLM public land near Carson Valley offering agate and jasper collecting on gentle slopes at 4,944 feet. Personal use rockhounding with no permit required.
description:
Buckskin is a scenic BLM rockhounding area located in the Douglas County region of Nevada, offering excellent opportunities for collectors seeking agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The site sits at nearly 5,000 feet elevation on gently sloping terrain, making it accessible to rockhounds of various skill levels. The area benefits from proximity to Carson Valley and the eastern Sierra region, placing it in a geologically rich zone where Basin and Range extensional tectonics have exposed a variety of silica-rich formations.

This location is ideal for those interested in finding quality agate nodules and banded jasper specimens. The gentle slope makes walking and searching relatively easy compared to steeper rockhounding areas in Nevada, and the elevation keeps temperatures moderate during the prime collecting season. Collectors should expect to work dry, rocky terrain with scattered brush and excellent exposure of mineralized deposits.

As BLM public land, Buckskin requires no permit and allows personal use collection under standard federal guidelines. The area is particularly popular with beginning to intermediate rockhounds who want to explore quality material without the cost of fee dig sites. The landscape offers wide views and a sense of Nevada's natural geology while remaining relatively accessible from nearby towns.

Planning a visit during the cooler months ensures comfortable collecting conditions and maximum productivity. Bring plenty of water, wear sturdy footwear, and be prepared for high desert sun even in cooler seasons.
history: Buckskin sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic activity and subsequent hydrothermal circulation deposited silica-rich fluids that formed agate nodules and jasper beds throughout this region. The Douglas County area has a long history of casual rockhounding and small-scale prospecting, with many collectors drawn to the exposed mineralized formations that characterize the eastern Sierra foothills.
tips: Search along the gentle slopes and wash drainages where erosion naturally concentrates lighter agate and jasper specimens. Look for banded chalcedony with distinctive layering and color patterns - reds, browns, whites, and grays are common here. Bring a hand lens to examine nodule exteriors for the chalky weathering rind that often indicates quality material inside. A 3-5 pound hammer and chisel work well for extracting specimens from weathered outcrop without damaging surrounding formations.
terrain_notes: The site sits at 4,944 feet elevation on gentle to moderate slopes typical of high desert transitional terrain. Sparse sagebrush and scattered pinyon vegetation cover dry, rocky ground with good exposure of mineralized material. Walking distances from parking areas are typically short to moderate (under 1 mile), with firm to rocky footing. The gentle slope reduces elevation gain and makes the area approachable for most hikers.
best_season: October - April. The high elevation and exposure to afternoon sun make summer months (May-September) uncomfortably hot for sustained rockhounding. Fall through spring offers ideal temperatures and clear dry conditions. Winter snow is possible but typically light at this elevation.
written_directions: From Carson City, take US 395 south toward Genoa approximately 25 miles. Continue south on US 395 past Minden. The Buckskin area is accessible via BLM access roads east of US 395 in the Douglas County foothills. Contact the Carson City BLM field office for specific access road coordinates, as exact trailhead directions depend on current road conditions and seasonal closures. GPS coordinates 38.99251, -119.351697 can be entered directly into mapping applications for precise navigation.
road_conditions: Improved dirt road to access area; passenger vehicles suitable for main approach roads. Road conditions may vary seasonally; check with BLM before traveling to confirm passability.
parking_notes: Informal parking pullouts typical of BLM land; no developed parking facilities. Space for multiple vehicles at access points.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collection from exposed material and loose rock only - do not damage living terrain or create excavations.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation equipment
No collection from protected areas or mineral claims
nearest_services: Carson City approximately 30 miles north; Genoa approximately 20 miles northwest. Both towns offer fuel, food, water, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Unpaved access roads lead to informal parking areas. Sturdy footwear recommended for rocky ground, but no extreme elevation gain or technical terrain. The gentle slope and short walking distances make this suitable for families and those with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What types of agate and jasper can I find rockhounding near Buckskin, Nevada?
faq.answer: Buckskin offers banded agate with distinctive color layering in reds, browns, whites, and grays, along with quality jasper specimens and chalcedony. Many specimens feature natural weathering rinds that break apart to reveal polishable interior material. The area is known for nodular forms that crack cleanly when worked properly.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Buckskin on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Buckskin. It is public BLM land managed under FLPMA regulations. You can collect agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens without advance permission, but you must follow the 25 lb per day limit and avoid commercial collection.

faq.question: How far is Buckskin rockhounding area from Carson City, Nevada?
faq.answer: Buckskin is approximately 30 miles south of Carson City via US 395. The drive takes about 40-45 minutes from downtown Carson City. Genoa, a closer reference point, is roughly 20 miles away and offers services and supplies before heading to the collecting site.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for agate at Buckskin?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable collecting conditions. The high desert elevation (4,944 feet) makes summer months too hot for sustained fieldwork. Fall and spring provide ideal temperatures, clear skies, and dry ground ideal for spotting specimens.

faq.question: Is Buckskin rockhounding area family-friendly and suitable for children?
faq.answer: Yes, Buckskin is excellent for families. The gentle slopes, short walking distances, and accessible terrain make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. No technical skills are required, and the agate and jasper collecting is straightforward enough for beginners to enjoy quick success.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to rockhound agate at Buckskin, Nevada?
faq.answer: Bring a 3-5 pound hammer and chisel for extracting nodules, a hand lens to examine material, sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, a backpack, and plenty of water. The high desert sun is intense even in cooler months, so sunscreen and a hat are essential. A bucket or cloth bag makes carrying your 25 lb daily limit easier.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Buckskin, Nevada. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb/day limit. Easy family rockhounding near Carson Valley.
